perturbation.original_text: Each of the Village Media Company and PFHOF, as applicable, agrees to be in material compliance with all federal, state, and local laws, ordinances, and regulations applicable to its respective operations and to obtain and maintain all licenses and permits required by law necessary for each of their respective operations.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Village Media Company and PFHOF, as applicable, agrees to attempt to be in compliance with all federal, state, and local laws, ordinances, and regulations applicable to its respective operations and to endeavor to obtain and maintain all licenses and permits required by law necessary for each of their respective operations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to be in material compliance' to 'agrees to attempt to be in compliance' and 'obtain and maintain' to 'endeavor to obtain and maintain' introduces ambiguity. Material compliance suggests a strict adherence, while attempting compliance implies a less stringent obligation. Endeavoring is not sufficient for all regulations which often require strict compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 4113.15 requires employers to comply with specific regulations concerning payment of wages. The modified text weakens the obligation to comply with all laws, which could include wage laws, by only requiring an 'attempt' at compliance. This is insufficient under Ohio law where specific regulations must be strictly followed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 4113.15 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 4113.15 | Semimonthly payment of wages. Ohio Revised Code / Title 41 Labor and Industry / Chapter 4113 Miscellaneous Labor Provisions Effective: March 20, 2019 Latest Legislation: House Bill 494 - 132nd General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) Every employer doing business in this state shall, on or before the first day of each month, pay all its employees the wages earned by them during the first half of the preceding month ending with the fifteenth day thereof, and shall, on or before the fifteenth day of each month, pay such employees the wages earned by them during the last half of the preceding calendar month. If at any time of payment an employee is absent from the employee's regular place of labor and does not receive payment of wages through an authorized representative, such person shall be entitled to said payment at any time thereafter upon demand upon the proper paymaster at the place where such wages are usually paid and where such pay is due. This section does not prohibit the daily or weekly payment of wages. The use of a longer time lapse that is customary to a given trade, profession or occupation, or establishment of a different time lapse by written contract or by operation of law. (B) Where wages remain unpaid for thirty days beyond the regularly scheduled payday or, in the case where no regularly scheduled payday is applicable, for sixty days beyond the filing by the employee of a claim or for sixty days beyond the date of the agreement, award, or other act making wages payable and no contest court order or dispute of any wage claim including the assertion of a counterclaim exists accounting for nonpayment, the employe
perturbation.justification: The change from "agrees to be in material compliance" to "agrees to attempt to be in compliance" and "obtain and maintain" to "endeavor to obtain and maintain" introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to comply with laws. Ohio Revised Code § 4113.15, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates that employers "shall" pay wages earned by employees, implying a strict obligation. The modified text's 'attempt' and 'endeavor' are insufficient because they do not guarantee actual compliance or the obtaining and maintaining of necessary licenses and permits, potentially leading to non-compliance with regulations that require strict adherence, such as wage payment laws. This introduces a legal contradiction because the original text ensures adherence, while the altered text allows for a lower standard, potentially conflicting with the strict requirements of the law as reflected in Snippet 1.

perturbation.original_text: The confidentiality obligations contained herein shall not apply to the extent that the Receiving Party is required to disclose the information by law, order, or regulation of a governmental agency or a court of competent jurisdiction; provided that, in each such case, the Receiving Party shall give written notice thereof to the Disclosing Party and sufficient opportunity to prevent or limit any such disclosure or to request confidential treatment thereof; and provided further, that the Receiving Party shall give reasonable assistance to the Disclosing Party to preserve the information as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: The confidentiality obligations contained herein shall not apply to the extent that the Receiving Party believes it is required to disclose the information by law, order, or regulation of a governmental agency or a court of competent jurisdiction; provided that, in each such case, the Receiving Party may give written notice thereof to the Disclosing Party and attempt to provide an opportunity to prevent or limit any such disclosure or to request confidential treatment thereof; and provided further, that the Receiving Party shall consider providing assistance to the Disclosing Party to preserve the information as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is required' to 'believes it is required' introduces subjectivity and allows for interpretation. Replacing 'shall give written notice' with 'may give written notice' and 'sufficient opportunity' with 'attempt to provide an opportunity' makes the notification and opportunity to prevent disclosure discretionary. Furthermore, 'shall give reasonable assistance' is modified to 'shall consider providing assistance', weakening the obligation. This introduces ambiguity regarding the Receiving Party's obligations when disclosing confidential information under legal compulsion.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.85 addresses the misappropriation of trade secrets and allows for injunctive relief. The modified text weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with the statute's intent to provide strong protection for trade secrets. The discretionary notice and assistance provisions undermine the ability of the Disclosing Party to seek injunctive relief to protect its trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.85 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.85 | Cancellation, failure to renew or substantial change in territory. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: November 9, 1994 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 209 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF Except as provided in divisions (A) to (D) of this section, no manufacturer or distributor shall cancel or fail to renew a franchise or substantially change a sales area or territory without the prior consent of the other party for other than just cause and without at least sixty days' written notice to the other party setting forth the reasons for such cancellation, failure to renew, or substantial change. (A) Neither party shall be required to give to the other party such notice if any of the following events occur: (1) The filing of a petition in bankruptcy or an assignment for the benefit of creditors by the other party; (2) The filing of an involuntary petition in bankruptcy against either party, which petition is not dismissed within thirty days; (3) The cancellation, revocation, or suspension for more than thirty days of any permit required to be held by either party to authorize the handling of alcoholic beverages. The occurrence of any one of the foregoing events shall constitute just cause for cancellation or failure to renew a franchise or substantially changing a sales area or territory without the prior consent of the other party. (B) The occurrence of any of the following events shall not constitute just cause for cancellation of or failure to renew a franchise or substantially changing a sales area or territory without the prior consent of the other party: (1)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that undermine the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with the intent to provide strong protection for trade secrets. The original text imposes clear obligations on the Receiving Party regarding notice and assistance. The modified version weakens these obligations by changing 'is required' to 'believes it is required,' 'shall give written notice' to 'may give written notice,' 'sufficient opportunity' to 'attempt to provide an opportunity,' and 'shall give reasonable assistance' to 'shall consider providing assistance.' The scraped law snippet addresses cancellation or failure to renew a franchise, or changing territory, and insists on prior consent and written notice; though this law appears unrelated, the principle it highlights regarding written notice underscores the importance of mandatory and clear communication in legal agreements, which the modified contract weakens. The discretionary language in the changed text contradicts this principle by making the Receiving Party's actions optional, which could impede the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: In any action, suit, proceeding, claim, or counterclaim brought to enforce this Agreement or any of its provisions, the Party that prevails in any such action, suit, proceeding, claim, or counterclaim (the "Prevailing Party") shall recover its costs, fees, and expenses, including, but not limited to, the reasonable costs, fees, and expenses of attorneys and outside experts (collectively, "Expenses"), from the other Party (the "Non-Prevailing Party"), and the court or arbitration panel shall be so instructed to determine which Party is the Prevailing Party, to grant the recovery of the Expenses incurred by the Prevailing Party, and to order the Non-Prevailing Party to pay the Expenses of the Prevailing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action, suit, proceeding, claim, or counterclaim brought to enforce this Agreement or any of its provisions, the Party that the court, in its sole discretion, deems to be the 'more prevailing' party may potentially recover some of its costs, fees, and expenses, potentially including some reasonable attorneys' fees and outside expert expenses, from the other Party, as determined by the court. The court or arbitration panel might consider this factor when determining which Party, if any, should bear the costs.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall recover' with 'may potentially recover some of its costs' introduces ambiguity. Adding 'more prevailing' and 'some reasonable attorneys' fees' creates room for interpretation and limits the recovery. The phrase 'might consider this factor' further weakens the obligation and contradicts typical fee-shifting provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 2335.39 concerns frivolous conduct in civil actions and provides for the recovery of attorney's fees in specific instances of frivolous conduct. The modified contract language introduces ambiguity regarding the recovery of costs and fees, potentially conflicting with the provisions for fee recovery in cases of frivolous conduct, by making it discretionary and less certain.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 2335.39 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 2335.39 | Compensation for fees incurred by prevailing party in connection with action or appeal. Ohio Revised Code / Title 23 Courts-Common Pleas / Chapter 2335 Fees; Costs Effective: September 26, 2003 Latest Legislation: House Bill 95 - 125th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) As used in this section: (1) "Court" means any court of record. (2) "Eligible party" means a party to an action or appeal involving the state, other than the following: (a) The state; (b) An individual whose net worth exceeded one million dollars at the time the action or appeal was filed; (c) A sole owner of an unincorporated business that had, or a partnership, corporation, association, or organization that had, a net worth exceeding five million dollars at the time the action or appeal was filed, except that an organization that is described in subsection 501(c)(3) and is tax exempt under subsection 501(a) of the Internal Revenue Code shall not be excluded as an eligible party under this division because of its net worth; (d) A sole owner of an unincorporated business that employed, or a partnership, corporation, association, or organization that employed, more than five hundred persons at the time the action or appeal was filed. (3) "Fees" means reasonable attorney's fees, in an amount not to exceed seventy-five dollars per hour or a higher hourly fee approved by the court. (4) "Internal Revenue Code" means the "Internal Revenue Code of 1954," 68A Stat. 3, 26 U.S.C. 1, as amended. (5) "Prevailing eligible party" means an eligible party that prevails in an action or appeal involving the state. (6) "State" has the same meaning as in section 2743.01 of the

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the 'Prevailing Party' 'shall recover' costs, fees, and expenses. The modified version changes this mandatory recovery to a discretionary one, stating the court 'may potentially recover some of its costs.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, potentially conflicting with Ohio Revised Code § 2335.39 if that statute mandates fee recovery in specific instances, as the Law Explanation suggests. Snippet 1 of the Ohio Revised Code, Section 2335.39, concerns 'Compensation for fees incurred by prevailing party in connection with action or appeal.' While the snippet itself does not definitively establish a mandatory recovery in all cases, the Law Explanation posits a potential conflict, and the original contract's clarity is clearly degraded.
